classifier_hint:
CRITICAL disambiguation rules:
- feat vs refactor: feat=ANY observable behavior change OR new public API; refactor=provably unchanged (same tests, same API). When in doubt, prefer feat.
- fix vs hotfix: hotfix=critical production emergency; fix=normal bug.
- fix vs security: security=proactive hardening, CVE patches, auth hardening; fix=non-security bugs.
- deps vs chore: deps=dependency version bumps only; chore=other maintenance (tooling, scripts).
- deps vs build: build=build system scripts/config; deps=bumping library versions in manifests.
- config vs chore: config=application/runtime config; chore=dev tooling and housekeeping.
- ux vs feat: ux=existing feature made easier/clearer; feat=new capability.
- init=bootstrap commit for a project or major subsystem; use once.
- wip=in-progress save-point; prefer a real type for finished commits.
- hack=deliberate temporary workaround; body must note intent to revisit.
- merge=merge/sync commits with no standalone logic change.

types.name: feat
types.description: New public API surface OR user-observable capability/behavior change
examples: Added pub fn process_batch() → feat (new API)
Migrated HTTP client to async → feat (behavior change)
types.hint: ANY observable behavior change OR new public API. When unsure between feat and refactor, prefer feat.

types.name: fix
types.description: Fixes incorrect behavior (bugs, crashes, wrong outputs, race conditions)
examples: Corrected off-by-one in pagination offset → fix (wrong output)
Guarded against panic on empty input → fix (crash)
types.hint: Non-security bug fixes. Security-motivated fix → security; production emergency → hotfix.

types.name: refactor
types.description: Internal restructuring with provably unchanged behavior
examples: Renamed internal module structure → refactor (no API change)
Extracted shared helper from three call sites → refactor
types.hint: Requires proof: same tests pass, same API. If behavior changes, use feat.

types.name: docs
types.description: Documentation only changes
examples: Expanded README usage section → docs
Added rustdoc examples to public API → docs
types.hint: Excludes prompt template files (prompts/*.md). Prompt changes are functional — use feat/fix/refactor.

types.name: test
types.description: Adding or modifying tests
examples: Added unit tests for parser edge cases → test
Migrated suite to table-driven cases → test
types.hint: Test-only changes. Production code touched alongside tests uses that code's type.

types.name: chore
types.description: Housekeeping: tooling scripts, editor config, miscellaneous maintenance not covered by other types
examples: Updated .gitignore and editor config → chore
Refreshed lockfile after unrelated change → chore
types.hint: Use deps for version bumps, config for app/env config, build for build scripts.

types.name: style
types.description: Formatting, whitespace changes (no logic change)
examples: Reformatted with rustfmt, no logic change → style
Normalized import ordering → style
types.hint: Variable/function renames are refactor, not style.

types.name: perf
types.description: Performance improvements (proven faster)
examples: Cached repeated lookups to cut allocations → perf
Replaced O(n^2) scan with hashmap → perf
types.hint: Requires evidence of a measurable improvement; speculative tidying is refactor.

types.name: build
types.description: Build system and toolchain configuration (build scripts, compiler/bundler settings)
examples: Added release profile to Cargo.toml → build
Wired a new build script step → build
types.hint: Build scripts and tooling config. Bumping library versions in manifests → deps.

types.name: ci
types.description: CI/CD configuration
examples: Added a test matrix to GitHub Actions → ci
Cached cargo registry between CI runs → ci
types.hint: Pipeline definitions and CI scripts. Runtime app config → config; IaC → infra.

types.name: revert
types.description: Reverts a previous commit
examples: Reverted commit abc123 introducing the regression → revert
types.hint: Undoing a prior commit. A fresh fix that supersedes it is fix, not revert.

types.name: deps
types.description: Dependency version bumps (Cargo.toml, package.json, go.mod, requirements.txt, etc.)
examples: Bumped serde 1.0.197 → 1.0.210 → deps
Upgraded transitive deps via lockfile → deps
types.hint: Version bumps only. Build system changes belong in build; lockfile-only changes can be deps.

types.name: security
types.description: Security hardening, CVE patches, auth improvements, input sanitization, rate limiting
examples: Sanitized user input before the SQL query → security
Added rate limiting to the login endpoint → security
types.hint: Use for proactive hardening too, not just bug fixes. Security-motivated fix → security, not fix.

types.name: config
types.description: Application or environment configuration changes (.env, settings, feature flags, runtime config)
examples: Added a feature flag for the new pipeline → config
Raised the default request timeout → config
types.hint: App/runtime config. Build system config → build; CI config → ci; dev tooling → chore.

types.name: ux
types.description: Usability and ergonomics improvements to existing interfaces (CLI flags, error messages, output formatting)
examples: Clarified an ambiguous error message → ux
Added a short alias for an existing flag → ux
types.hint: Existing feature made easier/clearer → ux. New capability → feat.

types.name: release
types.description: Version bump and release preparation (CHANGELOG.md updates, version files, release tags)
examples: Bumped version to 2.1.0 and updated CHANGELOG → release
Cut the v3 release notes → release
types.hint: Only for the release commit itself. Code changes alongside a release use their own type.

types.name: hotfix
types.description: Critical production fix requiring immediate patch, often on a dedicated hotfix branch
examples: Patched a production auth bypass on the hotfix branch → hotfix
Emergency rollback of a broken migration → hotfix
types.hint: Reserve for genuine production emergencies. Normal bugs → fix.

types.name: infra
types.description: Infrastructure-as-code changes (Terraform, Kubernetes manifests, Ansible, cloud config)
examples: Added a Terraform module for RDS → infra
Tuned Kubernetes resource limits → infra
types.hint: IaC and cloud provisioning. App config → config; CI pipelines → ci.

types.name: init
types.description: Initial commit bootstrapping a project, module, or major subsystem
examples: Scaffolded a new crate with cargo init → init
Bootstrapped the auth subsystem skeleton → init
types.hint: Use once per project/module bootstrap. Subsequent setup → chore or build.

types.name: merge
types.description: Merge or sync commit with no standalone logic change (merge branches, sync forks)
examples: Merged the release branch back into main → merge
Synced fork with upstream → merge
types.hint: Only when the commit is purely a merge. Squashed logic changes → use the appropriate type.

types.name: hack
types.description: Deliberate temporary workaround or shortcut with known technical debt
examples: Hardcoded a timeout pending the upstream fix → hack
Stubbed the flaky call behind a guard → hack
types.hint: Must signal intent to revisit in the body (e.g., TODO: replace once X lands).

types.name: wip
types.description: Incomplete in-progress work not ready for review or release
examples: Saved a partial refactor mid-progress → wip
Checkpointed exploratory spike → wip
types.hint: Prefer a real type for finished commits. Use wip only for explicit save-points.
